quiz_questions.question_text: The coefficient of variation (CV) is calculated as:

options.text: (Standard deviation / Mean) × 100%
(Variance / Mean) × 100%
(Range / Mean) × 100%
(Mean / Standard deviation) × 100%

quiz_questions.explanation: CV = (SD / Mean) × 100%, expressing the standard deviation as a percentage of the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: The main advantage of the CV over the standard deviation is that the CV:

options.text: Ignores the mean entirely and calculates variability without any reference to center
Only works with normally distributed data and cannot be applied to skewed data sets
Is unit-free, allowing comparison of variability between data sets with different units or scales
Is always smaller than the standard deviation regardless of the units or scale used

quiz_questions.explanation: Because the CV is a ratio of SD to mean, the units cancel, making it ideal for comparing variability across different measurement scales.

quiz_questions.question_text: Data set A has mean = 50 and SD = 10. Data set B has mean = 200 and SD = 30. Which has greater relative variability?

options.text: Cannot be compared
Set A, because CV = 20% > 15%
Set B, because SD = 30 > 10
They have equal relative variability

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_A = (10/50)×100% = 20%. CV_B = (30/200)×100% = 15%. Set A has greater relative variability despite having a smaller SD.

quiz_questions.question_text: A CV of 5% indicates that the data set has:

options.text: Low variability relative to the mean
A standard deviation of 5
A mean of 5
Very high variability relative to the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: A CV of 5% means the standard deviation is only 5% of the mean, indicating relatively low spread.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a data set has mean = 80 and SD = 12, the CV is:
The CV is NOT meaningful when:

options.text: The data set is large
The data set has outliers
The data are measured in percentages
The mean is zero or near zero

quiz_questions.explanation: When the mean is zero or very close to zero, dividing by the mean produces an undefined or extremely inflated CV.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lower CV indicates:

options.text: A larger sample size
Greater relative spread
Greater consistency relative to the mean
A higher mean

quiz_questions.explanation: A lower CV means the standard deviation is a smaller fraction of the mean, so data values are more consistently clustered around the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: The CV is expressed as a:

options.text: Logarithmic value
Percentage (or unitless decimal)
Raw number in the original units
Squared unit

quiz_questions.explanation: Because the units in SD and mean cancel, the CV is dimensionless and typically expressed as a percentage.

quiz_questions.question_text: If two data sets have the same mean but different standard deviations, the one with the larger SD will have:

options.text: An undefined CV
The same CV
A larger CV
A smaller CV

quiz_questions.explanation: With the same mean, CV is directly proportional to SD. A larger SD gives a larger CV.

quiz_questions.question_text: Machine A produces bolts with mean diameter 10 mm and SD 0.2 mm. Machine B produces bolts with mean diameter 50 mm and SD 0.8 mm. Which machine is more precise relative to its target?

options.text: Machine B, because 0.8 > 0.2
Machine B, because CV_B = 1.6% < CV_A = 2%
Machine A, because its SD is smaller
They are equally precise

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_A = (0.2/10)×100% = 2%. CV_B = (0.8/50)×100% = 1.6%. Machine B has less relative variability and is thus more relatively precise.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the coefficient of variation (CV)?

options.text: A measure of relative variability that expresses the standard deviation as a percentage of the mean
The square root of the variance, which converts squared units back to original units
The ratio of the mean to the median, used to measure the skewness of a distribution
The range expressed as a decimal, converting the difference between extremes to a fraction

quiz_questions.explanation: The CV = (SD/mean) × 100% is a standardized measure of dispersion that allows comparison across different scales.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'relative variability' mean?

options.text: The variability of a relative's data
The absolute range of the data
How spread out data are compared to the size of the mean, rather than just the absolute spread
The number of outliers in the data set

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative variability considers spread in proportion to the center, allowing meaningful comparisons when data sets have very different magnitudes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dimensionless' mean in statistics?

options.text: Having no physical size
A one-dimensional data set
Data that cannot be measured
A quantity with no units, formed by a ratio where units cancel

quiz_questions.explanation: The CV is dimensionless because dividing SD (in original units) by the mean (same units) cancels the units.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'precision' mean when comparing data sets using the CV?

options.text: How close the mean is to the true value, which reflects the accuracy of measurements
The number of decimal places used when reporting the results of statistical calculations
The closeness of repeated measurements to each other, reflected by low relative variability (low CV)
The sample size, which determines how many observations are collected in the study

quiz_questions.explanation: Precision refers to the consistency or repeatability of measurements. A lower CV indicates greater precision relative to the measurement scale.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'homogeneity' mean in the context of the CV?

options.text: Data are normally distributed
All data values are identical
Data values are similar in magnitude, indicated by a low CV
Data come from the same source

quiz_questions.explanation: A homogeneous data set has values clustered closely around the mean, resulting in a low CV.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'ratio scale' and why does it matter for the CV?

options.text: A nominal scale with categories labeled by numbers that have no meaningful numerical order
A scale with a true zero and meaningful ratios; the CV requires a ratio scale because dividing by zero or a meaningless mean is invalid
A scale that only applies to temperature measurements such as Fahrenheit and Celsius
Any scale that uses numbers, including ordinal, interval, and categorical numerical codes

quiz_questions.explanation: The CV divides by the mean, so the mean must be meaningful (not arbitrary). Ratio scales (e.g., weight, height, time) have a true zero, making the CV valid.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'heterogeneous' mean in the context of CV?

options.text: The data set has no standard deviation
Data values are all the same
Data come from mixed populations but have a low CV
Data values vary widely, indicated by a high CV

quiz_questions.explanation: A heterogeneous data set contains widely varying values relative to the mean, producing a high CV.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'scale-free comparison' mean?

options.text: Comparing data that have no scale labels
Comparing data sets without regard to their measurement units or magnitude, made possible by the CV
Using a bathroom scale to compare weights
Removing the mean from both data sets

quiz_questions.explanation: The CV enables scale-free comparisons by normalizing spread relative to the mean, removing the influence of different units or magnitudes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'reproducibility' and how does the CV measure it?

options.text: Having a large sample size, which ensures that the study has sufficient statistical power
The ability to get consistent results under repeated conditions; a low CV suggests high reproducibility
The ability to copy data files accurately from one storage location to another backup
A measurement made only once, without any repetition to verify the observed result

quiz_questions.explanation: In laboratory and manufacturing settings, the CV is commonly used to quantify reproducibility. Lower CV values indicate more consistent, reproducible results.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'normalized standard deviation' mean?

options.text: A standard deviation that has been rounded
The SD divided by the sample size
Another name for the coefficient of variation, since it normalizes the SD by dividing by the mean
A standard deviation calculated from a normal distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: The CV is sometimes called the normalized or relative standard deviation because it standardizes the SD by expressing it relative to the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lab measures the concentration of a drug in blood samples. Method A yields mean = 100 ng/mL with SD = 8 ng/mL. Method B yields mean = 500 ng/mL with SD = 25 ng/mL. Which method is more precise?

options.text: Method A, because its mean is smaller
Method B, because its SD (25) is larger
Method B, because CV_B = 5% is less than CV_A = 8%
They are equally precise

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_A = (8/100)×100% = 8%. CV_B = (25/500)×100% = 5%. Method B has lower relative variability, making it more precise.

quiz_questions.question_text: An agricultural researcher compares crop yields. Wheat: mean = 4 tons/hectare, SD = 0.6. Corn: mean = 10 tons/hectare, SD = 1.2. Which crop has more consistent yields?

options.text: Wheat, because its SD is smaller
Wheat, because it has a lower mean
Corn, because CV_corn = 12% vs CV_wheat = 15%
They are equally consistent

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_wheat = (0.6/4)×100% = 15%. CV_corn = (1.2/10)×100% = 12%. Corn has lower relative variability and more consistent yields.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher compares test score variability between two courses. Statistics: mean = 78, SD = 6. Art History: mean = 82, SD = 10. A parent asks which course has more uniform grading. What does the CV reveal?

options.text: They are equally uniform
Statistics is more uniform: CV ≈ 7.7% vs Art History CV ≈ 12.2%
Art History, because 10 > 6
Art History is more uniform because it has a higher mean

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_Stats = (6/78)×100% ≈ 7.7%. CV_Art = (10/82)×100% ≈ 12.2%. Statistics scores are more uniform relative to the mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: A financial analyst tracks two portfolios. Portfolio X: mean annual return = 8%, SD = 4%. Portfolio Y: mean annual return = 15%, SD = 6%. An investor wants the best risk-adjusted return. Which portfolio has less risk per unit of return?

options.text: Portfolio Y, because CV_Y = 40% vs CV_X = 50%
They have equal risk per unit of return
Portfolio X, because its SD is smaller
Portfolio X, because its mean is lower

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_X = (4/8)×100% = 50%. CV_Y = (6/15)×100% = 40%. Portfolio Y has less variability per unit of return.

quiz_questions.question_text: A quality control team measures the weight of cereal boxes. Line 1: mean = 500g, SD = 5g. Line 2: mean = 250g, SD = 3g. Management wants to identify which production line needs improvement. What does the CV suggest?

options.text: Neither needs improvement
Line 1 needs improvement because its SD (5g) is larger
Both lines are equally variable
Line 2 needs improvement because CV_2 = 1.2% > CV_1 = 1.0%

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_1 = (5/500)×100% = 1.0%. CV_2 = (3/250)×100% = 1.2%. Line 2 has slightly greater relative variability, indicating it needs improvement.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher measures the heights of adults (mean = 170 cm, SD = 10 cm) and the weights of newborns (mean = 3.5 kg, SD = 0.5 kg). She wants to compare variability. Why is the CV the appropriate tool?

options.text: Because the CV is always better than the SD
Because the means are different
Because the data sets use different units (cm vs kg) and different magnitudes, making SD comparison meaningless
Because both data sets are normally distributed

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_height = (10/170)×100% ≈ 5.9%. CV_weight = (0.5/3.5)×100% ≈ 14.3%. The CV allows valid comparison despite different units and scales.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemistry student runs an experiment 10 times and gets results with mean = 0.005 mol/L and SD = 0.003 mol/L. The CV is 60%. Why might this be problematic?

options.text: The CV cannot be calculated for such small numbers
A CV of 60% indicates extremely high relative variability, suggesting the experimental method lacks precision
A CV of 60% means 60% of the data are outliers
A CV of 60% is perfectly normal in chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: A CV of 60% means the SD is 60% of the mean, indicating measurements vary enormously relative to the average. The experiment lacks acceptable reproducibility.

quiz_questions.question_text: An HR department compares salaries in two departments. Engineering: mean = $95,000, SD = $12,000. Marketing: mean = $65,000, SD = $10,000. Which department has more salary inequality relative to its pay level?

options.text: Engineering, because higher pay means more inequality
Engineering, because its SD is larger
They have equal inequality
Marketing, because CV_marketing ≈ 15.4% > CV_engineering ≈ 12.6%

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_eng = (12000/95000)×100% ≈ 12.6%. CV_mkt = (10000/65000)×100% ≈ 15.4%. Marketing has greater relative salary spread.

quiz_questions.question_text: A climate scientist compares temperature variability in two cities. City A (tropical): mean = 28°C, SD = 2°C. City B (continental): mean = 15°C, SD = 8°C. Calculate the CV for each and identify which city has more temperature variability.

options.text: City A: CV ≈ 7.1%, City B: CV ≈ 53.3%; City B has far more relative variability
City A: CV = 14%, City B: CV = 50%
Both have the same CV
City A has more variability because tropical climates are unstable

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_A = (2/28)×100% ≈ 7.1%. CV_B = (8/15)×100% ≈ 53.3%. City B's continental climate produces much greater temperature variability relative to its average.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sports analyst compares the consistency of two basketball players' scoring. Player 1: mean = 22 points, SD = 4 points. Player 2: mean = 14 points, SD = 3 points. Which player is the more consistent scorer?

options.text: Player 2, because 3 < 4
Player 1, because CV ≈ 18.2% vs Player 2 CV ≈ 21.4%
They are equally consistent
Player 2, because lower mean means less variability

quiz_questions.explanation: CV_1 = (4/22)×100% ≈ 18.2%. CV_2 = (3/14)×100% ≈ 21.4%. Player 1 is relatively more consistent despite having a higher SD.
